name: Spaced Repetition + Testing Effect
overview: Two of the most replicated findings in cognitive psychology, often deployed together. The spacing effect (Ebbinghaus, 1885) shows that distributing study sessions across time produces dramatically better long-term retention than massed/cramming practice; Cepeda et al.'s 2006 meta-analysis of 317 experiments confirmed and quantified this. The testing effect / retrieval practice (Roediger & Karpicke, 2006) shows that actively retrieving information from memory is itself a learning event that strengthens later recall, dwarfing re-reading. Combined, they form the empirical foundation for every modern SRS (Anki, FSRS, Quizlet, Duolingo) and underwrite popular claims of 200-300% retention gains over cramming.
originator_or_company: Hermann Ebbinghaus (German psychologist, University of Berlin, 1885 - forgetting curve / spacing effect). Henry L. Roediger III & Jeffrey D. Karpicke (Washington University in St. Louis / Purdue University, 2006 - testing effect). Nicholas J. Cepeda, Harold Pashler, Edward Vul, John T. Wixted, Doug Rohrer (UCSD / USF / York University - 2006 meta-analysis and 2008 'temporal ridgeline'). Robert A. Bjork & Elizabeth L. Bjork (UCLA Bjork Learning and Forgetting Lab - 'desirable difficulties' framework that ties spacing and testing together).
key_resources: [Ebbinghaus, H. (1885). Memory: A Contribution to Experimental Psychology - English translation](https://psychclassics.yorku.ca/Ebbinghaus/index.htm)
[Roediger, H. L., & Karpicke, J. D. (2006). Test-Enhanced Learning. Psychological Science, 17(3), 249-255](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2006.01693.x)

[Roediger, H. L., & Karpicke, J. D. (2006). The Power of Testing Memory: Basic Research and Implications for Educational Practice. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 1(3), 181-210](http://psychnet.wustl.edu/memory/wp-content/uploads/2018/04/Roediger-Karpicke-2006_PPS.pdf)

[Cepeda, N. J., Pashler, H., Vul, E., Wixted, J. T., & Rohrer, D. (2006). Distributed practice in verbal recall tasks: A review and quantitative synthesis. Psychological Bulletin, 132(3), 354-380](https://augmentingcognition.com/assets/Cepeda2006.pdf)

[Cepeda, N. J., Vul, E., Rohrer, D., Wixted, J. T., & Pashler, H. (2008). Spacing Effects in Learning: A Temporal Ridgeline of Optimal Retention. Psychological Science, 19(11), 1095-1102](https://laplab.ucsd.edu/articles/Cepeda%20et%20al%202008_psychsci.pdf)

[Karpicke, J. D., & Blunt, J. R. (2011). Retrieval Practice Produces More Learning than Elaborative Studying with Concept Mapping. Science, 331(6018), 772-775](https://learninglab.psych.purdue.edu/downloads/2011/2011_Karpicke_Blunt_Science.pdf)

[Adesope, O. O., Trevisan, D. A., & Sundararajan, N. (2017). Rethinking the Use of Tests: A Meta-Analysis of Practice Testing. Review of Educational Research, 87(3), 659-701](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.3102/0034654316689306)
[Rowland, C. A. (2014). The Effect of Testing Versus Restudy on Retention: A Meta-Analytic Review. Psychological Bulletin, 140(6), 1432-1463](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25150680/)

[Bjork, E. L., & Bjork, R. A. (2011). Making things hard on yourself, but in a good way: Creating desirable difficulties to enhance learning](https://bjorklab.psych.ucla.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/13/2016/04/EBjork_RBjork_2011.pdf)
[Murre, J. M. J., & Dros, J. (2015). Replication and Analysis of Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve. PLoS ONE, 10(7)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4492928/)
core_mechanics: Three interlocking principles. (1) Forgetting curve (Ebbinghaus 1885): retention of newly learned material decays approximately exponentially - in his nonsense-syllable self-experiments roughly 42% was forgotten after 20 minutes, 56% after 1 hour, 67% after 1 day, 79% after 31 days. The curve flattens after each successful re-exposure, so well-timed reviews 'reset' the decay from a higher floor. (2) Spacing effect (Cepeda 2006 meta-analysis of 839 effects from 317 experiments): given a fixed total study time, distributing sessions across time produces better delayed retention than massing them. The 2008 'temporal ridgeline' paper (n>1350) added the critical empirical rule that the optimal inter-study gap is roughly 10-20% of the desired retention interval (e.g. for a 1-week test, review after ~1 day; for a 1-year test, review after ~3-5 weeks). This gap-to-test-delay ratio drives every modern SRS schedule. (3) Testing effect / retrieval practice (Roediger & Karpicke 2006): actively retrieving information modifies the memory trace itself, not just measures it. In their canonical experiment students who studied a prose passage once and then took three free-recall tests recalled 61% after 1 week, vs. 40% for students who re-read the same passage four times - despite the re-readers feeling more confident. Karpicke & Blunt (2011, Science) replicated the finding against elaborative concept-mapping, the then-best-practice. Adesope et al. (2017) meta-analyzed 272 effects from 188 experiments and found d = +0.51 for practice testing vs. restudy, d = +0.93 vs. no activity. Multiple-choice practice (d = +0.70) outperformed short-answer (d = +0.48). Mechanism: retrieval is a 'desirable difficulty' (Bjork) - the effortful search through memory strengthens the cue-target association, while passive re-reading produces fluency illusions (the feeling of knowing without the ability to recall). Combined design implication: present the right item at the right time AND make the user retrieve it, rather than re-read it. This is the algorithmic core of Anki/FSRS/SuperMemo.
designer_or_researcher_voice: Roediger & Karpicke (2006, Psychological Science): 'Although educators have long believed that taking tests improves retention, this belief has not had a strong influence on classroom practice. Our findings show that taking tests has a powerful effect on long-term retention.'
Roediger & Karpicke (2006, Perspectives on Psychological Science): 'Testing is a powerful means of improving learning, not just assessing it.'

Karpicke & Blunt (2011, Science): 'Practicing retrieval produces greater gains in meaningful learning than elaborative studying with concept mapping. The advantage of retrieval practice generalized across texts identical to those commonly found in science education.'

Robert Bjork (UCLA Bjork Lab): 'Conditions of instruction that make performance improve rapidly often fail to support long-term retention and transfer, whereas conditions that introduce difficulties for the learner - though frequently slowing the rate of apparent learning - often optimize long-term retention and transfer.'

Cepeda et al. (2008, Psychological Science): 'The interaction of gap and test delay implies that many educational practices are highly inefficient... The optimal gap value was about 20% of the test delay for delays of a few weeks, falling to about 5% when delay was one year.'

Hermann Ebbinghaus (1885, Memory): 'With any considerable number of repetitions, a suitable distribution of them over a space of time is decidedly more advantageous than the massing of them at a single time.'
metrics_or_evidence: Spacing effect: Cepeda 2006 meta-analysis - 839 effect estimates from 317 experiments, 184 articles; spacing reliably improves retention with effect size growing as test delay grows. The Donovan & Radosevich style classroom meta-analyses report d = 0.54 (95% CI [0.31, 0.77]) for distributed vs. massed practice. Cepeda 2008 (n=1350): optimal gap is 10-20% of retention interval. Testing effect: Roediger & Karpicke 2006 canonical result - 61% recall after 1 week with retrieval practice vs. 40% with restudy (a ~50% relative improvement on that delay). Adesope et al. 2017 meta-analysis - 272 effects from 188 experiments: d = +0.51 vs. restudy, d = +0.93 vs. no activity; multiple-choice practice d = +0.70, short-answer d = +0.48. Rowland 2014 meta-analysis (159 effect sizes): g = 0.50 vs. reading. Adesope/Phelps 2017 also reports g = 0.61 to g = 0.70. Bjork 'desirable difficulties' meta-analysis (29 studies): g = 0.74. The popularized '200-300% improvement' figure is widely cited in industry / EdTech blogs (e.g. for foreign-language vocabulary) but is a marketing-grade aggregation, not a single peer-reviewed effect size - the rigorous numbers are Cohen's d ~0.5-0.9 in classroom settings. [uncertain]
criticisms: (1) Domain narrowness: most spacing/testing research uses verbal recall of fact-like material (vocabulary, capitals, definitions). Transfer to ill-structured domains (essay writing, design judgement, clinical reasoning) is much weaker. (2) Not learning, only memorising: critics including Luca Lampariello and Hacker News commenters argue SRS optimises for recognition-of-isolated-items at the expense of contextual, generative knowledge - the 'isolated flashcard fallacy' (Andy Matuschak). (3) Replicability gaps in non-WEIRD samples and online unsupervised settings: a 2024 PMC study 'Testing the testing effect on prolific' failed to find the effect under low-effort online conditions. (4) Cost-benefit: testing effect requires effortful retrieval; if learners give up too easily, fluency-based study can be equal or better (Roediger himself notes this). (5) Negative-testing effect: tests with corrective-feedback failure can produce interference if the wrong answer is retrieved without correction. (6) The popular '200-300%' improvement claim is over-stated relative to the actual meta-analytic effect sizes (d ~0.5-0.9) which translate to roughly 20-50% relative gains in classroom contexts, not 200%. (7) Forgetting curves vary enormously between individuals and material types - one curve does not fit all (Murre & Dros 2015 replication confirmed the shape but not specific timings).
failure_cases: (1) Pure SRS dropout: roughly 80% of users abandon spaced-repetition apps within months (community surveys, reddit r/Anki / r/medicalschool), driven by uneven daily review counts and demoralising backlogs. (2) Re-reading textbooks for an exam outperformed by students using practice tests despite higher self-rated confidence in the re-readers - documented by Roediger/Karpicke 2006 and Karpicke/Roediger metacognition studies. (3) Cramming culture in Japanese exam prep (詰め込み): high short-term performance, near-total decay 30-90 days after the exam - the Ebbinghaus curve at population scale. (4) Concept mapping bubble (mid-2000s ed-tech) effectively dethroned by Karpicke & Blunt 2011 once head-to-head retrieval-practice comparisons were run. (5) Failed transfer cases in medical education where SRS-mastered facts do not show up in clinical reasoning OSCEs, prompting calls to combine SRS with case-based learning (multiple PMC reviews 2018-2024).
antipatterns: (1) Surfacing raw backlog counts as the headline daily metric - induces avoidance and quitting (the single biggest Anki-uninstall driver). (2) Penalising forgetting (streak loss / red animations on 'Again') - users start lying about recall, corrupting the schedule and the learner's metacognition. (3) Massing the user with a 200-card cold-start day after install - violates Cepeda's gap rule and feels punishing. (4) Equating 'I read it 5 times' with progress in the UI; this reinforces the fluency illusion the testing effect literature explicitly warns against. (5) Pushing dark-pattern reminders ('You will lose your progress!') - exploits loss aversion in a way Center for Humane Technology calls out. (6) Quoting the 200-300% figure in marketing copy unqualified; this is regulatorially risky in Japan under 景品表示法 (No.3 misleading-claim rule) when paired with a paid product. (7) Means-Ends Reversal: framing the 'desired retention %' as a quota the user is failing rather than as a forecast. (8) Putting raw FSRS / SM-2 parameter sliders in front of casual learners.
applicability_to_shikaq: Foundational. Spaced Repetition + Testing Effect is the empirical justification for shikaq's entire Practice tab and arguably the whole product thesis vs. '分厚い参考書 + 一夜漬け'. Concrete translation for the 20s-female / iPad-cafe / Apple-HIG persona: (a) Practice tab schedules questions with FSRS at 90% target retention, but presents it as a tasteful 'Today's 5 cards' card a la Apple News+ Featured - never 'You are 217 reviews behind'. (b) Every question is a retrieval event, never a re-read screen; even 'review' lessons start with a one-question recall before showing the explanation. (c) After a correct answer, show 'You'll likely remember this until <date>' (Apple Calendar / Reminders flavour) instead of XP numbers - leverages Cepeda's forecast intuition without gamifying. (d) Apple Fitness 3-ring on the Summary screen maps directly to the three constructs: Reviewed (volume) / Accuracy (testing-effect quality) / Streak (spacing consistency). HEXAD mapping: serves Achievers (mastery via measurable retention forecast), Philanthropists (the 'science says this works' angle - aligns with the cafe-study persona's identity), Free Spirits (configurable retention, optional cap), and Players (Fitness rings). NOT for Disruptors. (e) Pair with the existing AI Q&A screen so that questions the user gets wrong feed an automatic retrieval-practice queue, closing the loop between AI Q&A and Practice. (f) Use Liquid Glass tab bar for the review session toolbar; the four-button Again/Hard/Good/Easy maps to Apple Music's playback controls. (g) Critically: cap daily retrieval load (e.g. 30 cards default) to address the burnout antipattern - non-negotiable for the cafe persona. (h) Avoid putting raw 'forgetting curve' graphics on the marketing site - it triggers anxiety; instead show 'See you tomorrow at 8am' Calendar-style nudges.
phase_assignment: Phase 1 (3 weeks). This is theoretical bedrock - shikaq's claim to differ from 分厚い参考書 rests entirely on spacing + testing being implemented from day one. Without it, Practice is a generic quiz app and the marketing claim ('科学的に効率がいい') is unsupported. Implementation can lean on existing FSRS/SM-2 ports, so Phase 1 is realistic.
integration_points: DB: extend `questions` (already exists per db-schema.md) and add `card_states` (user_id, question_id, stability, difficulty, last_review_at, next_due_at, scheduled_days, reps, lapses, state) - shared with Anki/FSRS adoption. `quiz_results` is the substrate for retrieval-practice logging and feeds the FSRS optimizer. Components: Practice screen renders the daily retrieval queue; TodayContent shows due-today count + 'remember by' chip; Summary / Apple Fitness rings show retention forecast and streak; Sidebar 'Practice' badge shows capped due count. Edge Function (Supabase) for nightly per-user FSRS optimization once >=200 reviews exist. Hooks into AI Q&A: questions answered incorrectly auto-enqueue a card. RLS on card_states by user_id, admin bypass.
risks: (1) Over-claiming '200-300%' in marketing copy is a 景品表示法 (Japan Premiums and Representations Act) risk - Stripe / App Store reviewers also flag this. Use the conservative d ~0.5-0.9 framing internally; in marketing speak as 'efficient review' without false-precision percentages. (2) Theoretical purity vs. consumer UX trade-off: hardcore SRS practitioners (med students, language nerds) want full FSRS parameter control; the cafe persona wants none of it. shikaq must commit to the consumer surface, which means alienating the hardcore segment - acceptable. (3) The testing effect requires effortful retrieval, but multiple-choice with 4 options gives only ~2 bits of effort; consider mixing in cloze / short-answer formats for at least the post-lesson review, even though MC is operationally easier and shows higher meta-analytic effect size. (4) Cold-start: optimal spacing depends on stability estimates that require ~5-10 reviews per card. Use population FSRS defaults and avoid showing 'precision' UI until enough data accumulates. (5) Persona mismatch: spacing means days where the queue is bigger than yesterday - the cafe persona may interpret that as the app punishing them. Mitigate with daily cap + 'pause without backlog' mode. (6) Scope creep: don't build a custom scheduler; adopt ts-fsrs / py-fsrs.
apple_hig_compatibility: compatible. Both principles are invisible math; the rendering decisions are pure HIG. Spacing -> a single Apple News+ Featured card 'Today's 5 cards' on the Today tab. Testing -> question-first screens reminiscent of Journal's prompt-first composition. Retention forecast -> Apple Fitness 3-ring. Streak -> SF Symbol `flame.fill`. Audio quiz at night -> Apple Music Now Playing. The only HIG-conflicting upstream patterns (Anki desktop's parameter sliders, dense statistics, raw backlog counts) must be ruthlessly omitted. Liquid Glass works well for the review-session toolbar.
ethical_score: positive. These are arguably the cleanest learning-science findings to base a paid product on - the optimisation target (durable long-term retention with minimal time) is genuinely user-aligned, not engagement-for-engagement's-sake. Risks of burnout exist but are downstream of *presentation* (raw backlogs, streak punishments), not the algorithms themselves. shikaq can stay positive by capping daily review load, framing retention as a forecast rather than a quota, and refusing to gamify the failure events. No variable-reward loop, no time-after-time regret pattern, no ad-inventory pressure. Center for Humane Technology lens: passes.
evidence_quality: rigorous_RCT. Both phenomena have hundreds of replications including controlled lab experiments and field RCTs. Cepeda 2006 (317 experiments, 184 articles) and Adesope 2017 (188 experiments, 272 effects) are large rigorous meta-analyses. Karpicke & Blunt 2011 in Science is a signature high-quality replication. Murre & Dros 2015 directly replicated Ebbinghaus 1885. Some recent failures (Prolific online study 2024) show the effect is not invariant under low-engagement conditions, but the cumulative evidence base is among the strongest in cognitive psychology.
localization_fit_japan: high. Japanese qualification-exam culture (TOEIC, 簿記, ITパスポート, 基本情報) has explicit demand for 'すきま時間で効率よく覚える' which is precisely the spacing + testing pitch. The 20s-female cafe-study persona is already buying 一問一答 booklets that are crude paper-based testing-effect tools; shikaq's value is digitising and timing them properly. Japanese terms 「分散学習」「想起練習」are gaining mainstream traction in education-Twitter (#分散学習) and Zenn / note articles since 2020. The conservative legal frame matters: avoid loud 200-300% claims (景品表示法 risk) and instead use 'エビデンスのある復習タイミング' / 'テスト形式で覚える' which fit cultural expectations of restraint. Apple Health / Fitness is well-loved by this persona, so Fitness-style retention rings will land naturally.
